Like `Function`, `Layer` also used potentially duplicate values (e.g. Fn) extracted from `aws:cdk:path` for `arn`. Thus, the data structure was as follows, which could cause the same problem as with `Function`.

| LogicalId | arn | layer_id(SamResourceId) | name |
|-|-|-|-|
| aaaLayer123 | Layer | Layer | Layer |
| bbbLayer456 | Layer | Layer | Layer |
| cccLayer789 | Layer | Layer | Layer |

To deal with the same problem as `Function`, it was necessary to change the data structure to the following one.

| LogicalId | arn | layer_id(SamResourceId) | name |
|-|-|-|-|
| aaaLayer123 | aaaLayer123 | aaaLayer123 | Layer |
| bbbLayer456 | bbbLayer456 | bbbLayer456 | Layer |
| cccLayer789 | cccLayer789 | cccLayer789 | Layer |

However, the `layer_id` and `name` resolution logic was structured in the following order of reference.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layer_id -- refer if not --> name -- refer if not --> arn
```

Therefore, changing `arn` to a `logical_id` would change `name`, and specifying `name` would next change `layer_id`, which references `name`, so there was a need to specify each completely.

I think this change is necessary to have backward compatibility because it supported potentially duplicate values (e.g. Fn) extracted from `aws:cdk:path` as search conditions for `Layer`.

I'm looking into this. But this seems to be a weird API.

```C#
public abstract class PatternContext<TFrame> : IPatternContext
{
    protected TFrame? Frame;
```

doesn't have any constraints on `TFrame`. But the only usages of it use a `struct`:

https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/385fc8832b1d4160abe13645054b65458b6a12f8/src/libraries/Microsoft.Extensions.FileSystemGlobbing/src/Internal/PatternContexts/PatternContextLinear.cs#L10-L65

https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/385fc8832b1d4160abe13645054b65458b6a12f8/src/libraries/Microsoft.Extensions.FileSystemGlobbing/src/Internal/PatternContexts/PatternContextRagged.cs#L10-L111


I think what led to this is that the `protected TFrame Frame` field is not initialized in the ctor (and it doesn't have a `struct` constraint on it). So it needed to be `TFrame?`. Then in the derived classes, they read the field and pass it into `PushDataFrame`, and I think that's where the mistake happened of annotating `PushDataFrame(TFrame? frame)` as nullable.

I'll log a bug for this.

Yes, I understand that the existing code uses the `.python-version` file, but reviewing this pr and the pre-existing code, it looks like retrieving the version from the `.python-version` file is unnecessary.

It is only used as a _search string_ for correcting the bin_path:

```venv = bin_path[: bin_path.rfind(env_name)] + env_name```

In this case, it's isn't necessary to use the `.python-version`, and we can use the path discovered by `pyenv which python`, using the right most match.

Could we use something like this to bypass the need to use the `.python-version` file?

```
import re

python_version_match_regex = re.compile("(3\.\d+\.\d+)")

p = "/home/3.8.1/sample/.pyenv/versions/3.8.5/bin/python"
matches = [m for m in python_version_match_regex.finditer(p)]  # get all matches
match_count = len(matches)
last_match = matches[-1]
_, last_index = last_match.span()  # get position of last match
venv = p[:last_index]
```

Patroni not only creates files, but sometimes also directories (for example when copying logical slots to replicas).
In all cases umask and permissions should be set correctly and according to expectations of Postgres.
I.e., default values (owner only): https://github.com/postgres/postgres/blob/20c22333630111718bc97ce9a19b392f23e3c525/src/common/file_perm.c#L17-L25
and https://github.com/postgres/postgres/blob/20c22333630111718bc97ce9a19b392f23e3c525/src/common/file_perm.c#L37-L42 if group access is allowed.

Correct permissions should be "calculated" when:
1. We start Patroni with existing $PGDATA
2. After [bootstrap](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/1b6e23ab6a5a92892c7948afde415f10cb95acdb/patroni/postgresql/bootstrap.py#L290-L294) (initdb or custom bootstrap) and after [create_replica](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/1b6e23ab6a5a92892c7948afde415f10cb95acdb/patroni/postgresql/bootstrap.py#L278)

Great idea to move that behavior into a mixin, but there are some new problems now. Setting an attribute on self from a method on itself is a bit of a red flag, but it wasn't a deal breaker when it was just on the Team synchronizer. But now that `_m2m_has_changed` is on a mixin and `self.m2m_changed` is being set in multiple places, it is a problem.

We don't want to trust each class inheriting from `M2MAssignmentMixin` to correctly set `m2m_changed`. It would be better if `_m2m_has_changed` set `self.m2m_changed` inside the method when it is run. That way child classes only have to call `_m2m_has_changed` to test if that field has changed. Also, `_m2m_has_changed` should be changed to `m2m_has_changed`. Since its being called by child classes, its just best practice to not have it as a private method.

This pattern isn't very robust though, it works right now because we don't have any models with more than one m2m field, but as soon as that changes it will need to be updated. But, who knows how long that day will take to come. Maybe make a comment on the `M2MAssignmentMixin` that mentions this only works if the model has one m2m field.

As mentioned by @jarussi, I think this behavior would be misleading. Suppose I have a backend created with:
```python
CacheBackend.create(type='memcached')
```

If it inherits from `SingletonCreateMixin`, the next time I could call
```python
CacheBackend.create(type='redis')
```

And get the instance with `type='memcached'` (because it was created first). 

You could use the `*args` and `**kwargs` in the key for the singleton cache, but all parameters would have to be hashable. Another option would be calling `hash()` at the backend instance and use it as the singleton key. That would require the Singleton to implement `__hash__` properly (otherwise, the Singleton would not be a singleton).

Development versions in PEP440 are named `1.0.0betaX` or `1.0.0alphaX`.

So supposing that you have your `plugin.video.foo` in gotham (py3 compatible) with version 1.0.0:

version in gotham is `1.0.0`
version in matrix is `1.0.0+matrix.1`
version for development (external repo) is `1.0.1beta1`

I personally don't think there's a need to add `(https?:\/\/)?(www\.)?` as the regex will trigger without it when using `INVITE_URL_RE.search(text)` anyway and these regexes are supposed to be used as filters (they're in `redbot.core.utils.common_filters` namespace) so that seems like the most likely way this regex will be used in. With that said I'm not opposed to it (so you can keep it), I just don't find it necessary for the use cases they're intended for (filtering).

The additions of discord.io and discord.li are appreciated, though it would be better if you put TLDs into non-capturing group (`(?:gg|io|me|li)`) so that there are always the same amount of captured groups. I would also appreciate if you would send me some working invite link that uses discord.io and discord.li as I wasn't able to get hands on one myself.

As for the last part of the proposed regex (`.+[a-z]`), I think you should leave it as it was before (`(\S+)`), because:
a) it's useful to have the invite code in a captured group
b) it is prone to false-positives like `discord.gg/ abc`
c) it may capture incomplete invite url when it doesn't end with a letter, e.g. `discord.gg/kPdts7`

Alternatively, you could make the regex more strict by acknowledging what characters are allowed for each service:
- `discord.gg`/`discordapp.com/invite` - A-Z, a-z, 0-9
- `discord.me` - A-Z, a-z, 0-9 and `-`
- `discord.io`/`discord.li` - unknown

This is only really possible if you can narrow down possible characters for discord.io/discord.li and I don't think there's much point in being strict when regex's intended usage is filtering so I myself would just prefer `(\S+)`

So in the end, the regex would be:
```suggestion
INVITE_URL_RE = re.compile(r"(discord\.(?:gg|io|me|li)|discordapp\.com\/invite)\/(\S+)", re.I)
```
or if you think we should keep the optional https/www so that the regex could possibly be used in some other situations (doing a match against full string perhaps?) then it would be (using non-capturing groups for `http(s)` and `www`):
```suggestion
INVITE_URL_RE = re.compile(r"(?:https?:\/\/)?(?:www\.)?(discord\.(?:gg|io|me|li)|discordapp\.com\/invite)\/(\S+)", re.I)
```

Combined with the way we a repo is cloned in _bootstrap: `git clone -b <rev> -- <url> <dest>`,
resetting this way, will result the repo to still be refer to the original checked out branch, however, now containing the FETCH_HEAD code.
This causes the the local branch to differ from upstream, see below.

I suggest that when bootstrap inits (clones) the remote repo, then we create and switch to local special branch, i.e. similar to what is done for the project, where `manifest-rev` is local only branch, and we go into detached head.

Example, seen from user:
```
$ west init --mr work_branch_1  <-- starting with work_branch_1
=== Updated configuration written to /projects/github/west_test/west/config ===                        
=== Running 'west update --reset-manifest --reset-projects' to update repositories ===                 
=== Fetching and resetting manifest (west/manifest/) to 'work_branch_1'                                
                                                                                                       
$ pushd west/manifest/                                                                                 
$ git status                                                                                           
On branch work_branch_1   <-- We are at local branch work_branch_1, which tracks origin/work_branch_1
Your branch is up-to-date with 'origin/work_branch_1'.                                                 
nothing to commit, working directory clean                                                             
$ popd                                                                                                 
                                                                                                       
$ west init --mr work_branch_2                                                                         
=== Updated configuration written to /projects/github/west_test/west/config ===                        
=== Running 'west update --reset-manifest --reset-projects' to update repositories ===                 
=== Fetching and resetting manifest (west/manifest/) to 'work_branch_2'                                
                                                                                                       
$ pushd west/manifest/                                                                                 
$ git status                                                                                           
On branch work_branch_1 <-- We are still at work_branch_1, which tracks origin/work_branch_1, but now with content of work_branch_2, which could be very different content.                                                                               
Your branch and 'origin/work_branch_1' have diverged,                                                  
and have 1 and 1 different commit each, respectively.                                                  
(use "git pull" to merge the remote branch into yours)                                                 
nothing to commit, working directory clean                                                             
$ popd                                                                                                 
```

So I took a stab at this.  Making the `update_root_hash()` method exclusively responsible for calculating all hashes is a bad idea, for two reasons:

* Doing so is at odds with point (3) in your review below.  The methods that alter the Trie structure will need to update the TrieCursor with information on the node(s) whose hashes are now invalid.

* Trie update methods like `splice_leaf()` and `promote_leaf_to_node4()` touch two or more branches, meaning that the branch information would need to be conveyed via the TrieCursor to `update_root_hash()`.

I think the most all-around reasonable thing to do in order to ensure that all modified nodes' and their parents' hashes are recalculated exactly one is to design the Trie mutation methods to guarantee that the _highest_ node they touch will have the correct hash when the method returns (which implies that they will ensure that all nodes beneath it have the correct hashes).   
 I think this is appropriate because these methods _already know_ which nodes' hashes need to be recalculated.  This is more or less how they work today, but there are two instances where a node's hash is calculated twice (which I have fixed).  Then, `update_root_hash()` only needs to know the highest node modified in order to update the root hash.

Per point (4) below, I think that this gets us the an acceptable correctness test -- an auditor would need to confirm that:

* All mutation methods render the sub-Trie beneath the highest-modified node well-formed (i.e. all nodes have the right hashes).  This is trivial for leaf insert/replace, and relatively straightforward to confirm for node-promotion and path-splicing.
* `update_root_hash()` re-calculates all node hashes starting from the parent of the root of the modified sub-Trie.

Just in case the purpose isn't obvious to everyone, let me explain so you all can provide some guidance on how I could improve the comments/docstrings or if we should maybe rework the current structure to be a bit more transparent as to why the code is designed the way it is (apologies in advance for the verbosity):

Normally a `ManagedArray`'s data is structured as `outer_ptr1->inner_ptr1->data1`. If you pass around `ManagedArray`s by value, you create other instances, where each instance has the structure `outer_ptrX->inner_ptr1->data`, which means that any of them can resize/reallocate/whatever the data and all the others will still be pointing to the right data (and same goes for updating the size).

Now, what we want is that if compute is called and there are numpy arrays referencing the current data, we need to keep that data exactly where it is because the numpy arrays are only passed the raw pointer to that data. The first line of `deepCopy` creates a new `ManagedArray` which now contains `outer_ptr2->inner_ptr2->data2`. The assignment above then sets `inner_ptr2 = inner_ptr1`, so the pointer sequence is now `outer_ptr2->inner_ptr2->data1` (and `data2` goes out of scope and is freed). We can now safely call `reallocate` on the original array, at which point we now have `outer_ptr[1|X]->inner_ptr1->data3` while the original `ManagedArrayManager` (which had its reference count incremented for every numpy array created) now contains the `ManagedArray` with the pointer sequence `outer_ptr2->inner_ptr2->data1`, so it's keeping the original data alive and is the only pointer to it.

Hopefully you already got that from the code and were just clarifying the poor naming, but if not there's my super long description.

The `listdir` method which was implemented using the old version of the azure storage library, used the `list_blobs` method in that old library. That method in the old library had a [delimiter](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/list-blobs#uri-parameters) parameter, which helps in listing directories much easier. For example if we have two directories in the container, say `foo/` and `bar/` each of which could have hundreds of thousands of blobs, we would not want to list all of them in the `list_blobs` operations. Instead if you specify `delimiter='/'`, you would only be returned `['foo/', 'bar/']` in the `list_blobs` operation. The new version of the azure storage library seems not to have the `delimiter` option in the `list_blobs` function. Instead there is a function [walk_blobs](https://azuresdkdocs.blob.core.windows.net/$web/python/azure-storage-blob/12.5.0/azure.storage.blob.html#azure.storage.blob.ContainerClient.walk_blobs) which has this parameter. I think that function would be more appropriate here instead of `list_blobs`. I think just replacing `self.client.list_blobs(name_starts_with=dir_path)` with `self.client.walk_blobs(name_starts_with=dir_path, delimiter='/')`, should work.

A few reasons:

- This is the standard way to annotate instance attrs in Python-- see [PEP 526](https://peps.python.org/pep-0526/#class-and-instance-variable-annotations). There is a special `ClassVar` annotation you use for class variables.
- IMO it is a lot more readable than annotations in `__init__.

> I'm surprised the types didnt propagate from __init__ correctly though, i guess issues witch check calls?

- Yes, the types are often not inferred correctly in `__init__`. I _think_ it would work the same as the above style if we explicitly annotated the type for every assignment in `__init__`, but I'm not 100% sure. Regardless, it's a lot harder to read/find the declared type when its embedded in often lengthy `__init__` logic.
- The above style matches the style used by dataclasses and pydantic, which I still hope to transition to

The conditional property function returns a dictionary of facts about a project. The `skip_if` and `remove_if` entries of any node in the `todos` tree contain conjunctive normal form (ANDs of logical operations) that operate on the facts returned from conditional property function to determine whether to skip or remove their part of the tree.

That answers what the `condition_property_function` is. We don't have to import/export it because we're only import/exporting the `todos` tree (and logical operators), not the fact-returning function.

> They are performed on a single node, but this is because you may download multiple potentially distributed blocks onto it.

But these blocks are isolated to the trainer's shard of the source dataset, while the distributed shuffle operation pulls samples from the global dataset, which is another sense in which this shuffle is "local", i.e. local to the trainer's shard.

> I'd say it's solving distributed shuffle by turning it into non-distributed, rather than actually performing local shuffle.

By that logic, Datasets has no "local" operations, e.g. batching can happen across blocks but in my opinion happens "locally" since the actual batching operation happens in the consumer's iterator. In terms of implementation, since this shuffle fundamentally fills a local buffer and pops random samples from it, I think it's local in that sense.

I think that "distributed" vs "local" might be the wrong distinction, I've mostly been thinking about this in terms of "global" vs "local", where we're talking about the quality of the random sampling and whether data is mixed across trainer shards (global data mixing). There's also the distinction of "global" vs "local" in terms of the blocks within a trainer's shard, i.e. whether we mix samples over time or if we only pick random samples from each block: this shard-local shuffle is global within the shard, but it's also not a perfectly uniform shuffle of the shard unless the memory buffer is as large as the shard.

Anyway, what are you pushing for here? That we clarify that samples are mixed from the entire shard, over time?

Hmm I'm not sure if casting this as "single-column" vs "multi-column" is the best way to frame this, since we're now trying to hide the former as an implementation detail for a "collection of tensors" concept. We haven't successfully hidden it completely, the returned schema still shows that it's a single-column table, but I think that's still the goal.

The way I've been thinking about it is that we have a "tensor dataset" concept that represents a "collection of tensors", and it uses a single-tensor-column table under-the-hood as it's concrete data representation, but all APIs to interact with the data should provide a tensor (i.e. NumPy) API. This is addressing the pure-tensor (e.g. only images) use case.

I also think that the more common multi-column use case is a mix of column types (e.g. N feature columns, one or more of which are tensor columns, and a non-tensor label column), not multiple tensor columns. So this single-tensor-column vs multi-tensor-column framing doesn't seem to line up with the underlying use cases.

I think that the ideal framing would be "tensor datasets", consisting of a single-tensor-column table that presents a "collection of tensors" concept and API to the user, and "tensors in tabular datasets" or "tensors in tables", where a table can contain one or more tensor columns along with other column types. What do you think?

I considered a protocol, especially since the helper rules don't really use many attributes of the `PythonToolRequirementsBase`. The problem comes when they forward to `GeneratePythonLockfile.from_tool`, which references many other attributes of `PythonToolRequirementsBase` (some of which are computed and reference even more). Some of these are exclusively lockfile related (`lockfile`, `uses_custom_lockfile`) which could be pulled out or naturally fit here. But others were more general, like `interpreter_constraints` or `all_requirements`. You end up depending on the entirety of `PythonToolRequirementsBase`.

Thinking along that line leads to the idea that maybe `PythonToolRequirementsBase` is already the Interface. It has many helpers to work with a lockfile already on it. Some of these do more than just option handling: `pex_requirements` looks similar to a mirror version of `GeneratePythonLockfile.from_tool`. So maybe it makes sense to pull `PythonToolRequirementsBase` and the helper rules out into one file, where they can mutually reference each other. 

Thinking about it more, though, if we moved `GeneratePythonLockfile.from_tool` onto `PythonToolRequirementsBase`, we could then have a protocol that was just
```python
class PythonToolRequirementsBase(Protocol):
    """Break the import cycle."""
    options_scope: str
    lockfile_rules_type: LockfileRules
    def to_generate_python_lockfile(...) -> GeneratePythonLockfile: ...
```
which actually seems pretty good. Thanks for encouraging me to have a second look at this. I hope this can be done without a breaking change to the plugins API, it seems to be used by every Python tool. I'll try to see if it works out.

Along the line of defining the Protocol and `LockfileRules` separately, if we split `PythonToolBase` and `PythonToolRequirementsBase`; and registered the rules in `PythonToolBase`; we could also linearise this. That's how it worked on my first pass at this, the concrete tool depended on the helpers and `PythonToolRequirementsBase`; and the helpers depended on `PythonToolRequirementsBase`

Thanks for sharing your surprise. Surely this would need some improved documentation to illustrate how `files()` now accepts an `Anchor` (instead of `Package`), where the anchor indicates:

- the container (importer) from where resources can be discovered,
- if a module is indicated, that's equivalent to indicating the container of that module,
- if a top-level module is indicated, that will anchor on resources reachable at the top level by that same importer.

This is the behavior [indicated by pkg_resources](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/pkg_resources.html#basic-resource-access):

> Note that if a module name is used, then the resource name is relative to the package immediately containing the named module.

However, I've also observed that the pkg_resources implementation also allows for loading resources against unpackaged modules. And while that behavior may have been unintentional, it works because it's a natural consequence of relying on the importer/loader to resolve the resources from the same location as the indicated package/module. That is, because the importlib mechanism supports loading modules from outside packages, so too should resources be loadable from that location.

`logging.basicConfig` [configures the root logger](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.basicConfig). Subsequent calls to basicConfig are ignored.

With this addition, if the schedule library is imported at the top of the file, the root logger is configured using the schedule `basicConfig`. Now, if the the program itself calls `basicConfig` to configure the root logger, that config will be ignored because it has already been configured by schedule. This is an example of such:

```
import logging
import schedule

logging.basicConfig(format='CUSTOMFORMAT: %(message)s', level=logging.DEBUG)

logging.info("test")
```
I would expect this code to print `test`, but with the proposed change it doesn't print anything. 

This will be quite confusing for users. Therefore I'm proposing to remove this `basicConfig` call and merge the pr without it. Let me know what you think!

```python
    def calcparams_cec(self, effective_irradiance, temp_cell, **kwargs):
        """
        Use the :py:func:`calcparams_cec` function, the input
        parameters and ``self.module_parameters`` to calculate the
        module currents and resistances.
        Parameters
        ----------
        effective_irradiance : numeric
            The irradiance (W/m2) that is converted to photocurrent.

            See pvsystem.calcparams_cec for details

        kwargs = _build_kwargs(['a_ref', 'I_L_ref', 'I_o_ref', 'R_sh_ref',
```

If you did work out a way to store the executable bit in the AWS FileInfo, then you would have the AWSJobStore be responsible for keeping track of that bit, probably instead of making the FileID do it.

That would let you set the executable bit properly when people give you strings instead if FileIDs when they download files, but it would be a different design than the one you've been implementing so far, where the FileID keeps track of executability, so I wouldn't do it.

These `_importFIle` and `_exportFile` overrides in `AWSJobStore` have one purpose, which is to make copying more efficient when you are importing from something on AWS to an `AWSJobStore`, or exporting from an `AWSJobStore` to somewhere else on AWS. Instead of downloading the file from AWS to the local machine and then uploading it elsewhere on AWS, they detect AWS-to-AWS operations and ask AWS to just copy the data internally.

Since files on AWS have no concept of executability, there's no execute bit to read when you import from AWS, and no execute bit to set when you export to AWS. So you shouldn't need to touch the AWS `_importFile` and `_exportFile` overrides in any substantial way; they should just not set the execute bit in the FileIDs they produce for imports from AWS, and not do anything with the execute bit in the file IDs they get to export to AWS.

If you are having trouble with imports and exports between AWS and local files, you might need to look at `_importFile` and `_exportFile` as defined in the `AbstractJobStore` (which is being called from here when the other side of the copy is not on AWS), or in the `FileJobStore`.

I believe multiprocessing with use os.fork() on unix systems and thus we can take advantage of COW to reduce ram usage. However AFAIK on Windows child process will reimport all module level imports and thus may require some extra ram. But I don't think the extra imports will create a big burden on the ram usage( or maybe our scheduler box is just too big :P). I'll add an entry in UPDATING.md regarding this config line.

About the SQLA connection, you actually have the point. On Windows we might ended up configuring extra 16 connection pool while reimporting. And since subprocesses spun up by multiprocessing module do not run atexit() we might leave some hanging connections there in theory. However from my observation and test, SQLA initializes connections lazily and thus we at most have empty pool in the subprocesses.

The kwargs are actually needed if this reader (file handler) is loaded at the same time as other readers. This is an unfortunate side effect of how the Scene handles reader keyword arguments and we've done it in other readers. The Scene, when given a dictionary of keyword arguments, will pass them to all readers being loaded. If a file handler/reader doesn't have `**kwargs` then any unrecognized keyword arguments will raise an exception.

Recently @gerritholl added the ability to specify the exact reader you want to pass keyword arguments to from the Scene `__init__` so maybe this isn't required anymore, but I'd be nervous about deprecating it too fast. I could remove it for this reader with the understanding that I only need to handle `mask_saturated` and the assumption that the base HDFEOS file handler has no other keyword arguments. I could then remove the `**kwargs` that I added to the base HDFEOS file handler above.

1) at least with the public APIs available now, once you turn it on it's on for the entire python interpreter permanently, which could negatively impact any usage of tf.Session() elsewhere in the TB backend e.g. PersistentOpEvaluator
2) eager mode requires going back-and-forth from python to C++ for every op and my understanding is that this can often actually be slower than graph mode, though in this particular case we're just executing one op so it may not matter

by "central polling" I mean one polling loop for all consumers (strategy, error handling, etc.) vs. each of (strategy, error handling, etc.) calling executor.status() -> provider.status() on their own.

Anyway, the scheme in this PR is fundamentally single-threaded. There is one thread that maintains the status of the system and it dispatches that status to various consumers who deal with a snapshot of the status of the system. It's true that it requires some help from the providers in determining how often certain things should be done.

(1) We could keep the polling loop as it is, remove PollItem and get_polling_interval() methods, implement a cache as above, and have the polling loop call status() on all providers at some regular interval that is smaller than the smallest polling interval (this will be a somewhat magical decision, since the smallest polling interval would not be known to the loop). This is almost fine.

(2) We could also abandon the polling loop entirely and have the strategy and whatever other entities call status() on their own, knowing that the caching decorator does the right thing. The above decorator does not do the right thing. It does not deal with being invoked from multiple threads. If invoked in quick succession without having a cached result, it will pass all the calls through. A proper implementation would determine if an invoking thread is the first to trigger an update, stall all other calling threads while the update happens, and finally return the cached result to all waiting threads when it becomes available. That's not that hard to implement, but it's nowhere near the simplicity of not implementing it at all. The cost, I think, is much higher than explicitly exposing the polling interval. We could, of course, also go with a good enough implementation that doesn't do things quite right. I think that's just incurring technical debt. We'll end up with a method (status()) that seems OK to call freely, but it isn't quite so, and the problems arising from it would be subtle and hard to figure out once we forget about this little conversation. It would be like a button that says "press me", but which would, occasionally, make things fail in subtle ways. In other words, not a good idea. So anyway, I think we'd trade explicitness for hidden complexity. I'd rather have the former.

Even if we keep one loop (1), I don't think that's much different than (2). It would still involve a button that says "press me" which either fails in subtle ways or it's not worth making it function correctly.

This is useful both for mypy and as documentation!

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
from numpy.testing import assert_allclose

def softmaxcrossentropy(x, target, weight=None, reduction='mean'):  # type: (np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray
    max_x = np.max(x, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    exp_x = np.exp(x - max_x)
    p = exp_x / np.sum(exp_x, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    inp = np.log(p)
    input_shape = inp.shape
    if len(input_shape) == 2:
        N, C = input_shape
        neg_gather_element_input = np.zeros((N, ), dtype=np.float32)
        for i in range(N):
            neg_gather_element_input[i] = -inp[i][target[i]]
    if len(input_shape) == 3:
        N, C, D = input_shape
        neg_gather_element_input = np.zeros((N, D), dtype=np.float32)
        for i in range(N):
            for d in range(D):
                neg_gather_element_input[i][d] = -inp[i][target[i][d]][d]

    loss = neg_gather_element_input
    if weight is not None:
        gather_weight = np.take(weight, target)
        loss = gather_weight * loss
        if reduction == 'mean':
            return loss.sum() / gather_weight.sum()

    if reduction == 'mean':
        loss = np.mean(loss)
    if reduction == 'sum':
        loss = np.sum(loss)

    return loss

loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
input = torch.randn(3, 5)
target = torch.empty(3, dtype=torch.long).random_(5)
output = loss(input, target)

my_loss = softmaxcrossentropy(input.numpy(), target.numpy())
assert_allclose(my_loss, output.numpy(), rtol=1e-05)

Bryant,
The sole purpose of `feed_keys.yaml` is to provide to the feed requiring it (at the moment only WHALE_ALERT) a `key` class parameter without requiring creation of an object.
The sole need is to be able to call the class method `info` without more parameter.
If we move this config file at `FeedHanlder` level, then this need is not satisfied. `key` cannot be made a class parameter of `Feed` class.

In this case, the other approach to have `info` class method working (that I just realize now) is simply to add a `key_id` parameter to that function:
```python
@classmethod
def info(cls, key_id: str = None) -> dict:
```

I can do that, but then I don't need any longer a config file.
So, if you agree, if you feel that a config file for `Feed` class is not appropriate (and I have no trouble with that, no worries), I will simply remove it, and do as proposed above: add a `key_id` parameter to `info` class method.

IOW, if using pants as a library, pants is coming from its wheel. The proper dependency is already expressed and the full pants binary contents are in your chroot by virtue of this. $ unzip -qc pantsbuild.pants-1.20.0rc0-cp36-abi3-manylinux1_x86_64.whl pantsbuild.pants-1.20.0rc0.dist-info/entry_points.txt
```
[console_scripts]

```

> I was just considering the cases where double executions of the same processor with overlapping pages are submitted. That would mostly end in error, wouldn't it? Unless overwrite is already set for the next requests.

Oh that. Yes, that's right. But for that we don't have to do anything (detecting overlaps etc) – this error will be coming from the processor itself (it already contains logic looking for conflicts in the output fileGrp / page list).

> > Why make this contingent on the identity of the processor?
> 
> Because the workspace will be unlocked for the same processor requests that potentially can have different page ranges. The collisions for the same processor but different pages are handled by the METS server. However, if we get a different processor but the same pages, then the workspace is locked and that request goes into the waiting internal queue (CACHED). Consider cases when running processors cannot be done independently, i.e., the output of the first processor has to be used by the second processor.

This sounds confusing to me. My [above analysis](https://github.com/OCR-D/core/pull/1069#discussion_r1259789028) still stands IMHO – I don't see any reason why we should look at the identity of the processor. (But I agree with @kba that if we do, then it would really have to be the combination of processor and parameters.) It's the just pages and the fileGrps that cause a dependency.

Regarding timeouts (using the actual number of pages in the Processor Server model, adding a timeout mechanism in the Processing Worker) – should we track that in a separate issue?

You might consider wrapping this comment into a `usage()` function, so that this information can be shared with the hapless typo-ing user on the command line.  Consider:
```python
def usage(exc: Optional[Exception] = None):
    print("Usage: {} <mapping_path> <function_path> <args_path> <result_path>", file=sys.stderr)
    print("\n[Brief description of what this script does]", file=sys.stderr)
    print("  - mapping_path - path to a file containing a pickled dictionary ...", file=sys.stderr)
    print("  - function_path - ...", file=sys.stderr)
    
    if exc:
        print(f"\n({type(exc).__name}) {exc}", file=sys.stderr)

    sys.exit(1)  # non-zero exit code to represent "not successful" to the OS
```

Yeah, the assumption was that developers would want their changes forward-ported on updates (at least when working on the main branch), unless suppressed with `--no-update`. A merge conflict is a much more informative thing to give them than a rolled-back repository in that case, and doesn't touch their work. It felt like "hey, we know you want to update, but we're gonna remove the standard Git mechanism for dealing with merge conflicts".

It's all kinda moot with the `--ff-only` though. Now we assume that people never want to automatically forward-port changes instead. That solves the problem with work branches and is simple to get right, so it's probably better.

I agree that we should try to make it so that most users never have to touch the west/manifest repositories.

Could do this later maybe, if we ever need it. Theoretically, you might need to skip the west update if the manifest update fails too, in case backwards-incompatible changes are made to the manifest format.

It's simple to solve manually if you assume that people tinkering with the west and manifest repositories know what they're doing.

One setting may be useful to you when using conda init:

```
auto_activate_base: bool
```

This setting controls whether conda activates your base environment when it first starts up.  You'll have the "conda" command available either way, but without activating the environment, none of the other programs in the environment will be available until the environment is activated with ``conda activate base``.  People sometimes choose this setting to speed up the time their shell takes to start up, or to keep conda-installed software from automatically hiding their other software.

Ok so this is difficult. The short answer is because dep_names is `{"buildozer", "python-for-android", "setuptools", "wheel"}`.

The long answer is, any outside entity (as which I pretend to be testing, I'm not saying that is reasonable but that was the idea) should approach this with "I use setuptools to build, so `setuptools` should be in there" (with `include_build_requirements=True`), and then there's of course the other direct deps `buildozer` and `python-for-android`. For items that **I care about** (as a user) that would be `{"buildozer", "python-for-android", "setuptools"}` which is what the test verifies.

Now that `wheel` is added is due to the way pep517 operates which essentially needs it for building the metadata - that knowledge doesn't fall from the sky though, that is done manually in `pythonforandroid/pythonpackage.py`, *but* it feels like knowledge I just felt shouldn't be something the outside user worries about or something I would want to need to keep in sync as such an internal detail, and therefore I didn't want to test it here.

Hi @cwhanse, @wholmgren , & others,

Not a blocker to me, but curious if you had considered other numerical differentiation tools like:
* [`scipy.misc.derivative`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.misc.derivative.html#scipy.misc.derivative) - allows you to change the number of points used (from 3 to 5, etc) as long as it's odd - check the [source](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.5.2/scipy/misc/common.py#L75-L145) to see the weights used
* [`statsmodels.tools.numdiff`](https://www.statsmodels.org/stable/tools.html#numerical-differentiation) - also does complex numbers and can return the gradient, Jacobians, and Hessians
* [`numdifftools`](https://numdifftools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) - latest release was June, 2019, but this is based on the [MATLAB derivest](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/fileexchange/13490-adaptive-robust-numerical-differentiation) originally authored by legendary Kodak mathematician [John D'errico](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/profile/authors/869215)
* [algopy](https://pythonhosted.org/algopy/) - I believe there used to be more packages like this, but Algopy seems to be the only one (in Python) that has survived. It requires significant effort, and overloads NumPy, but I believe it calculates derivatives of each operation as it occurs rather than of functional outputs

Anyway, just putting this out there, I don't actually expect you (or MetPy) to change anything, just always curious how or why there are so many tools that seem to all do the same thing, in apparent contradiction to [pep20: the Zen of Python](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/):
>There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.

I don't love that this would just through a `KeyError` if someone used the wrong `curve_name`. I think we'll have that problem in a number of places.

Let's think about alternatives to this setup. Maybe something like:

```py
class Curve(NamedTuple):
  field_modulus: int
  fq12_modulus_coeffs: Tuple[int, ...]
  fq_modulus_coeffs: Tuple[int, int]

bls12_381 = Curve(
  field_modulus = 21888242871839275222246405745257275088696311157297823662689037894645226208583,
  fq2_modulus_coeffs = (1, 0),
  fq12_modulus_coeffs = (2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, -2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0),  # Implied + [1]
)
```

Which makes this method something like:
```py
def __init__(self, coeffs: Sequence[IntOrFQ], curve: Curve) -> None:
  super().__init__(coeffs, curve, curve.fq12_modulus_coeffs)
```
Of course, it comes at the downside of having to import the curve to create an `FQ`. But for the caller, it means fewer magic strings and less likelihood for a typo with a confusing error. For the library, hopefully better readability (at least this method got simpler).

>> avoid encoding quantiles in strings

> Let's start with this simpler conversation

Oh, no, I do mean _in_ strings and not _as_ strings (which is not an obvious distinction and I apologize) -- I was trying to imagine how I'd prefer to do this and I had the same concerns you identify about the ergonomics of indexing a dictionary with a float, since floats are lies and we can't have nice things, so representing decimal values as strings is `this_is_fine.gif`. Parsing them out of texty labels is the only thing that seems annoying.

> I was tossing up between a dict or Series. Here are the features that drove me to use a Series:
> * I want values to be ordered (grouping related quantities together and ordering withing those groups) and keyed
> * I want the keys to be flexible (an arbitrary list of quantiles might be supplied)
> * Batch mode will smash a bunch of these return values together, and the user should be able to conveniently address the collection of results either by row or column (i.e. get all quantiles for one set of samples, or get one quantile across all sets of samples). To me this really smells like a DataFrame.
> Does that go some way to convince you that a Series is better here than an OrderedDict, dict or custom type?

I think I agree with your design goals; I'm tripping over the flat indexing scheme, since it feels like we "really" have a handful of different kinds of numbers rather than a single collection of numbers. One consequence is that I don't agree that the current elements of the omnibus Series have a natural total ordering and I don't really understand why you'd want to maintain one, except for display, and you can provide that with a nice `__str__` method.

I think a useful concrete alternative would be an object including `relative_uplift` and `absolute_uplift` members of type Series, indexed by the string "expectation" or str(quantile), which I think meets the latter two goals. The batching would upzone those into one DataFrame each (and a Series for each of the scalar values you want to include).

> Have your thoughts wandered to anywhere stronger than "wonder"?

I've gotten used to the idea now and you convinced me that there's a natural use case for specifying them as quantiles and I'm convinced I can serialize that in a useful way; I think specifying as quantiles makes sense.

Hey,

I understand the importance of large tensors and I'm really looking forward to them as well. Don't get me wrong, I'm not underestimating the work that comes with supporting them.

We already had a history of issues that were caused by too large allocations. Thus, for the sake of keeping CI stable (independent on whether we're running on per-PR or nightly) and to adhere to a high test standard, I'd prefer if we would not delay adressing the test strategy.

I think there's a misunderstanding. With my proposed approach, you would still be able to make a full test of a single operator. But instead of randomly generating the input tensor, you would use a generator model that gives you predictable and procedually generatable input tensor instead of a large and randomly generated one.

The idea here would then be to give this procedually generated input tensor to the operator and since it's a predicutable input, you can also have a predictable output that you can verify. On the way, the operator will run as usual, it's just that the source of the data is not physical memory but generated values. 

The only case that wouldn't be supported is the chaining of operators since that would require storing the intermediary values. 

The idea here is that (in whatever fashion you'd like) we generate fake input and output tensors that return predictable and valid data, but doesn't actually consume memory on the RAM. From MXNets perspective, it shouldn't matter where the real values is coming from. For the sake of an operator, it's important that it actually retrieves proper values and that would be the case here.

I had to go and verify myself how the versions were parsed again. Here's a summary and new versions we'd like to have:
```
typed | sysver | parsed                              | new parsed
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
 yes  | >= 3.8 | ast: sysver down to 3.5, typed: 2.7 | ast: sysver down to 3.3, typed: 2.7
 yes  |  < 3.8 | typed: 3.7, 3.6, 2.7                | typed: 3.7 down to 3.3, 2.7
  no  | >= 3.8 | ast: sysver down to 3.5             | ast: sysver down to 3.3
  no  |  < 3.8 | error on import
```
So what I've understood is that the builtin ast is good for all versions except 2.7 if we're above 3.8, but below it we need `typed_ast`. 1. The number of pairs in an n-set is `comb(n, 2) = n! / (2! * (n-2)!) = (n * (n-1)) / 2`, so `pairs_number` is exactly the number of pairs in `conflicting_rules`
2. Shift/Shift conflict arise if two rules could be matched in the same context. In other words, the conflicting rules are descendants of the starting rules in the pseudo-tree representing the relations between the rules. In particular, every pair taken from `conflicting_rules` has at least one common ancestor: the starting rule.
3. `ancestors` will eventually contain all the ancestors of each of the conflicting rules. Indeed, at the beginning of each step `i >= 0`, all of the ancestors of `rule` at a distance  `<= i` of `rule` are contained in `ancestors[rule.origin]`
4. `ancestors_intersection` builds the sets of common ancestors of rules given as arguments

These points prove that there are always exactly `pairs_number` pairs (tell me if one of them is not obvious). However, point 2 assumes that it is proven that Lark provides a valid LALR parser. For a non-complying parser (for example one which would find a conflict for rules that are not effectively used, and which could then be in different connected components of the rules graph), there is no guarantee on the termination of this loop.

If you want me to support non-complying parsers, we could brutally majorise the number of steps with `len(conflicting_rules)`. This has the drawback of hiding potential flaws in other parts of the parser.

In my view, SRP and DND-SD while related are two different protocols and functions. SRP client is used by device to publish its services. DSN-SD client is used to discover services within the home/network. It can be true that we may end up running SRP server and DNS server on same device (on a BR), but we should not assume or rely that they are always on same device (specially from client perspectives). These are two different network entities. 

I think it is the job of a DNS name server to be able to answer questions related to all domains/services it is managing (how it knows and collects the service info, e.g. through interacting with SRP servers or mDNS or ... should not matter to clients that are using it).

That said, we already have API to get the selected server currently being used by SRP client (when auto-start is being used). {"items":["## **My running bundles**","These are bundles that are currently running or queued to be run.","a","b","d","c",""],"after_sort_key":-28,"ids":[676,677]}

sort keys can be assigned negative numbers, which is why *2 can mess up the ordering, because we place smaller sort_key in front and larger sort_key on the back, if the sort key was assigned negative number, *2 makes it smaller and move it to the front and thus the mess up.

Example: 
row 1: key -27
row 2: key -28
we want to insert an item between row 1 & row 2, the original code will cause the row 2 key be -56 + 1 = -55 and thus will be placed before row 1 after insertion

So the original code was a bug and removing *2 is good.

The signature for the multiplication operator has a lot of moving pieces. It starts with a `ITransformer[_Leaf_T, Tree[_Leaf_U]]` instance (a transformer that converts a `Tree[_Leaf_T`] to `Tree[_Leaf_U]`). Then a `ITransformer[_Leaf_U, _Return_V]` instance (a transformer that converts a `Tree[_Leaf_U`] to `_Return_V`).  Which results in a `ITransformer[_Leaf_T, _Return_V]` (a transformer that converts a `Tree[_Leaf_T`] to `_Return_V`).

There isn't a technical reason why it needs to be named with `_Return_V` instead of `_Return_U`. However, I was thinking that using `_Return_V` would make it clearer that the final type isn't necessarily related to the intermediary `_Leaf_U`. I'm open to switching to `_Leaf_U` or putting some of my above description into a comment in the source code if you think the current state should be changed.

The actor based compactor tends to be more performant, as long as it doesn't exhaust object store memory and fail completely. So the task-based compactor tends to be slower but more resilient to failure.

Related to this, I have another example compactor I've developed that just uses the actor to hold S3 file-refs, and kicks off the dedupe task to eagerly listen to, read, and concatenate intermediate S3 files coming out of the hash_bucket step. The HashBucket actor is also modified to contain a "finalized" flag that is set once all hash-bucketing tasks complete, so that the dedupe task knows when to stop listening and proceed to sorting/deduplicating (@edoakes has also mentioned that [Async Actors](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/async_api.html) should help clean up the finalization sleep/poll step I currently use). 

So far, this seems to provide the best combination of performance and resilience. Here's the relevant code:

```python
def compact(
        input_bucket,
        output_bucket,
        table_stream_id,
        primary_keys,
        sort_keys,
        max_records_per_output_file,
        num_hash_buckets):

    # check preconditions before doing any computationally expensive work
    check_preconditions(
        primary_keys,
        sort_keys,
        max_records_per_output_file,
    )

    # define distinct, but constant, pk hash and event timestamp column names
    col_uuid = "4000f124-dfbd-48c6-885b-7b22621a6d41"
    pk_hash_column_name = "{}_hash".format(col_uuid)
    event_timestamp_column_name = "{}_event_timestamp".format(col_uuid)

    # append the event timestamp column to the sort key list
    sort_keys.append(event_timestamp_column_name)

    # first group like primary keys together by hashing them into buckets
    input_file_paths = filter_file_paths_by_prefix(
        input_bucket,
        "input/{}".format(table_stream_id))
    all_hash_bucket_indices = set()

    hb_actors = []
    for i in range(num_hash_buckets):
        hb_actors.append(HashBucket.remote())

    # create dedupe tasks listening for datasets to compact in each hash bucket
    dd_tasks_pending = []
    for hb_index in range(num_hash_buckets):
        dd_task_promise = dedupe.remote(
            output_bucket,
            table_stream_id,
            hb_index,
            pk_hash_column_name,
            sort_keys,
            max_records_per_output_file,
            hb_actors[hb_index],
        )
        dd_tasks_pending.append(dd_task_promise)

    # group like primary keys together by hashing them into buckets
    hb_tasks_pending = []
    for input_file_path in input_file_paths:
        hb_task_promise = hash_bucket.remote(
            [input_file_path],
            output_bucket,
            table_stream_id,
            primary_keys,
            num_hash_buckets,
            pk_hash_column_name,
            event_timestamp_column_name,
            hb_actors,
        )
        hb_tasks_pending.append(hb_task_promise)
    while len(hb_tasks_pending):
        hb_task_complete, hb_tasks_pending = ray.wait(hb_tasks_pending)
        hash_bucket_indices = ray.get(hb_task_complete[0])
        all_hash_bucket_indices.update(hash_bucket_indices)

    # finalize hash bucketing so that all dedupe tasks flush their pending work
    for hb_index in all_hash_bucket_indices:
        hb_actors[hb_index].finalize.remote()

    # wait for all pending dedupe tasks to complete
    ray.get(dd_tasks_pending)


@ray.remote
class HashBucket:
    def __init__(self, ):
        self.file_paths = set()
        self.finalized = False

    def append(self, file_path):
        self.file_paths.add(file_path)

    def get(self):
        return self.file_paths

    def finalize(self):
        self.finalized = True

    def is_finalized(self):
        return self.finalized


@ray.remote
def hash_bucket(
        input_file_paths,
        output_bucket,
        table_stream_id,
        primary_keys,
        num_buckets,
        hash_column_name,
        event_timestamp_column_name,
        hb_actors):

    # read input parquet path into a single dataframe
    dataframe = read_files_add_event_timestamp(
        input_file_paths,
        event_timestamp_column_name,
    )

    # group the data by primary key hash value
    df_groups = group_by_pk_hash_bucket(
        dataframe,
        num_buckets,
        primary_keys,
        hash_column_name,
    )

    # write grouped output data to files including the group name
    hash_bucket_indices = []
    for hash_bucket_index, df_group in df_groups:
        hash_bucket_indices.append(hash_bucket_index)

        output = drop_hash_bucket_column(df_group)

        output_file_path = get_hash_bucket_output_file_path(
            output_bucket,
            table_stream_id,
            hash_bucket_index)
        output.to_parquet(output_file_path, flavor="spark")
        hb_actors[hash_bucket_index].append.remote(output_file_path)

    return hash_bucket_indices


@ray.remote
def dedupe(
        output_bucket,
        table_stream_id,
        hash_bucket_index,
        primary_keys,
        sort_keys,
        max_records_per_output_file,
        hb_actor):

    input_file_to_df = {}
    observed_file_paths = set()
    finalized = False

    dedupe_out_file_prefix = get_dedupe_output_file_prefix(
        output_bucket,
        table_stream_id,
        hash_bucket_index,
    )

    # read any previously compacted parquet files for this bucket
    prev_deduped_df = read_parquet_files_by_url_prefix(dedupe_out_file_prefix)
    input_file_to_df[dedupe_out_file_prefix] = prev_deduped_df

    while not finalized:
        finalized = ray.get(hb_actor.is_finalized.remote())

        # run one post-finalization concat to catch the last set of files
        input_file_paths_promise = hb_actor.get.remote()
        input_file_paths = ray.get(input_file_paths_promise)
        new_file_paths = input_file_paths.difference(observed_file_paths)
        if len(new_file_paths):
            input_file_to_df.update(read_parquet_files(new_file_paths))
            observed_file_paths.update(new_file_paths)
        time.sleep(1)

    # concatenate all input dataframes
    dataframe = pd.concat(input_file_to_df.values(), axis=0, copy=False)

    # sort by sort keys
    dataframe.sort_values(sort_keys, inplace=True)

    # drop duplicates by primary key
    dataframe.drop_duplicates(primary_keys, inplace=True)

    # write sorted, compacted table back
    dedupe_out_file_prefix = get_dedupe_output_file_prefix(
        output_bucket,
        table_stream_id,
        hash_bucket_index,
    )
    write_parquet_files(
        dataframe,
        dedupe_out_file_prefix,
        max_records_per_output_file,
    )
```

Also, we change the hash bucket file prefix to put its output in a separate "intermediate" folder:
``` python
def get_hash_bucket_file_prefix(table_stream_id, hash_bucket_index):
    return "intermediate/{}_{}_".format(table_stream_id, hash_bucket_index)
```

Ensuring that the first `Dataset` is released after execution/consumption of the full pipeline starts (i.e. `.iter_datasets()` is called) is a strong requirement here, since the core `DatasetPipeline` semantics are that datasets are aggressively cleaned up as soon as the pipeline consumer has moved past the dataset. All `DatasetPipeline` use cases rely on these semantics, which is why a `DatasetPipeline` can only be read once in general.

There are two questions here:
1. Do we need to support `pipe.schema()` after the full pipeline has already started being consumed?
2. If so, how would we support it?

Given that users typically query the schema _before_ starting to iterate over the pipeline, the answer to (1) may be no. I can't immediately think of why the user would want to poke at the schema during execution;  `.iter_datasets()` is generally the "sink" of the pipeline. And if they really wanted to poke at the schema, they could get the most up-to-date schema by inspecting the schema for the most recently yielded dataset from `.iter_datasets()`.

Assuming that the answer to (1) is yes, two options immediately come to mind for supporting it:
1. We always peek when starting full pipeline consumption and cache the schema. This is simple, but is a less general solution if we want to calculate other things off of the peeks (e.g. other metadata).
2. We always keep a reference to the last computed dataset. This initially sounds good, but this will prevent us from being able to release the last dataset in the pipeline while we're computing the next dataset, which is a nonstarter.

We could try out the first solution or we could have `.schema()` throw an error that you can't fetch the schema of a pipeline that's currently being consumed. I'm leaning towards the latter I think.

This method allows the labels in an encoded name in `message` to be read and compared one by one.

One thing to have in mind (and is different with labels vs full name) is that as we read and compare the labels, the offset can jump back in the message  in the case of compressed name.

I considered different models on how to update/manage the `aOffset` here:
1. If the label does not match, we skip over the label and update the offset to end of current label. But this did not make sense to me:
   - Why would the user want to skip over just a label if it does not match. It makes more sense for user to want try to compare label with maybe something else (in this case offset should remian the same)
   - `aOffset` would not be tracking end of original name field sicne we may have jumped back in message as we read labels one by one.
 2. If the label does not match, we skip over the entire name field. This makes more sense, but implementing it complicates the method and its use:
    - Now we need the user to provide multiple offsets as paramters to this method. The offset to start of the original name field in addition to offset to current label to read (just so that we skip over the original name filed if the label does not match).
    - This makes this method more complicated to use for a situation which may not that common/useful
       - If users want this behavior they can keep track of original offset of name themselve and then use `ParseName()` to parse and skip over it.
3. I decided on this model which made most sense to me. It's simple and practical (i.e, we do not update the offset if the label does not match).

So there's two things going on here:
1. Resolvers that resolve to `None` are removed from their container. 
2. `!no_value` is a resolver that exclusively resolves to `None` without any other functionality. 

It works just like `AWS::NoValue`, which is pretty useful in CloudFormation templates where you have logic like:
`!If [ 'MyCondition', !Ref MyVPCId, !Ref AWS::NoValue ]`. In a CloudFormation template, that condition would result in no value being set on that key if 'MyCondition' was equal to False.

In the context of Sceptre, imagine this scenario:

```yaml
parameters:
    my_parameter: {{ var.get('some_value_that_might_not_be_set', '!no_value' }}
```

Imagine the CloudFormation template has a default value for my_parameter. But if you pass any string at all for that parameter, that default value will not actually be used and it will just use the parameter value you've passed. But by using `!no_value` in this way, we won't actually pass "my_parameter" at all to the template if "some_value_that_might_not_be_set" is not set on the `var`.

Could you accomplish the same via other means in Jinja? Sure. Would it be more elegant? It depends on the way the user is setting up their Stack and StackGroups. `!no_value` gives the user an option that they can use. It gets more important when you talk about inheriting things from StackGroups.

I think the important point is we want to quickly identify the set of unique multicast addresses. My proposal was to have a bit for each address that indicates whether the given multicast address is "authoritative" or "duplicate". When sending MR.req message, we only consider and maintain state for "authoritative" IPv6 addresses. This should avoid the O(N^2) time complexity and result in O(N) time complexity. Of course, it means that adding/removing a multicast address also has O(N) time complexity.

The bit-vector simply makes it a bit more efficient by avoiding the walk across all child address entries, but it's still O(N) time complexity.

The job() method returns a python generator (iterator protocol).  It does one of three things.

1. yield a Job object (which has a run() method)
2. yield None (there's nothing to do right now, but there might be something to do in the future
3. return and end the iterator (the operation is completed)

The job object is invoked at some point in the future.  In particular, with the multi-threaded executor, it goes into a queue.

Meanwhile, we keep calling the iterator to get more work.  For example, a scatter step is going to produce N jobs, an then idle (return None) until all those jobs have completed.

At the top level of the workflow, we loop over each step and the ones which are ready to run, we call the job() method to ask that step to generate jobs for us.  If that step returns None we'll go on to the next thing, but we'll eventually loop around back to iterate on the step again.

It might be helpful to think of these iterators as channels feeding job objects up to the top level.  Part of the process is going through each iterator and asking it if it has new values to produce or not (sort of like reading a channel, except that we are actually passing control to the iterator which has to yield it back).

Per https://docs.taskcluster.net/docs/manual/tasks/groups, schedulerId _can_ be used to control who can manipulate tasks, but projectId is preferred (in fact, projectId was invented because schedulerId was doing too many things and impractical for this purpose).

That said, I think it makes sense to make this method parallel to [queue.cancelTask](https://docs.taskcluster.net/docs/reference/platform/queue/api#cancelTask):

any of
* queue:cancel-task:<schedulerId>/<taskGroupId>/<taskId>; or
* queue:cancel-task-in-project:<projectId>;
* all of
  * queue:cancel-task; and
  * assume:scheduler-id:<schedulerId>/<taskGroupId>

The third of those ("all of..") is deprecated, so no need to duplicate that. You've said you'll introduce project-id support later, which will correspond to the second. So, I think it makes sense to mirror the first option here, which is option 1 above.

This allows a few things:
 * `queue:$verb-task-group:*` -> cancel/seal anything you want
 * `queue:$verb-task-group:tc-github/*` -> cancel/seal tasks created by tc-github
 * `queue:$verb-task-group:$schedulerId/$taskGroupId` - permission to cancel this specific task group. This might be granted in temporary credentials, for example.

We could also do away with this if we made `Metrics.merge_with()` mutate the `self` `Metrics` rather than it returning a new merged copy, then we could just pass a `DataMungingMetrics` handle around... But I feel like this is more implicit collection and therefore error prone, and is also less extendable than a `MetricsCollector` abstraction.

The problem is `name`:
super().__init__ calls the Keithley init, which puts "name" into the kwargs (as it does not know a "name" parameter), then it calls Instrument.init with the name argument twice (once from Keithley init code and once from the kwargs), which raises an Exception.
There are a few options to circumvent this problem:

- Add `kwargs.setdefault('name', "Keithley 2260B DC Power Supply")` in Keithley's init before it calls super and remove the `name=Keithley` part from the super().__init parameters. That way the instrument is called Keithley unless you give it a name via kwarg (as you do it here). (My recommendation)
- call super().__init__ in the Texio init, but without a name parameter. Set `self.name = "Texio...."` after calling super().__init__
- You could call `super(Keithley2260B, self).__init__(`, because that way you get the parent of the Keithley class.

We can only check their non-default values. This means that users need to specify explicitly all types rather than doing something like `fill=defaultdict(lambda: 0, {features.Mask: 255}))`. @ericl Yep, and agreed that the cleanest solution would involve (1), but one idea for implementing (4) + (5) now (without executor control loop integration) would be doing a `ray.wait()` check at pick time:
```python
def add_actor(self, actor: ray.actor.ActorHandle):
    self._pending_actors[actor.ready.remote()] = actor

def pick_actor(self) -> ray.actor.ActorHandle:
    if self._pending_actors:
        # At least one actor still pending, block until at least one actor is ready,
        # and check if any pending actors are newly ready.
        it not self._tasks_in_flight:
            # No actors ready, block until at least one actor is ready.
            self._wait_for_ready_actors(block_for_one=True)
        # At least one actor ready, quickly check if more actors are now ready.
        self._wait_for_ready_actors(block_for_one=False)

    actor = min(
         self._tasks_in_flight.keys(), key=lambda actor: self._tasks_in_flight[actor]
    )
    self._tasks_in_flight[actor] += 1
    return actor

def _wait_for_ready_actors(self, block_for_one: bool):
    ready, _ = ray.wait(
        list(self._pending_actors.keys()),
        num_returns=1 if block_for_one else len(self._pending_actors),
        fetch_local=False,
        timeout=None if block_for_one else 0)
    for future in ready:
        actor = self._pending_actor.pop(future)
        self._tasks_in_flight[actor] = 0
```

```
kwargs
            Extra parameters passed to the relevant functions. Climatological
```

[This stackoverflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62511086/how-to-document-kwargs-according-to-numpy-style-docstring) does give some more insight that could be useful.

```
setup.py:3: error: Skipping analyzing "setuptools": module is installed, but
missing library stubs or py.typed marker  [import]
    from setuptools import setup

src/prettytable/colortable.py:6: error: Cannot find implementation or library
stub for module named "colorama"  [import]
        from colorama import init
```

This is because we're importing the setuptools and Colorama modules but they don't have type hints (their tracking issues: https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/2345, https://github.com/tartley/colorama/issues/206).

However, just like PrettyTable didn't type hints before https://github.com/jazzband/prettytable/pull/205, there are stubs published by Typeshed and available at https://pypi.org/project/types-colorama/ and https://pypi.org/project/types-setuptools/

So we can make use of those by telling pre-commit to install it like this:

```suggestion
      - id: mypy
        additional_dependencies: [types-colorama, types-setuptools]
```

(If running mypy outside of pre-commit, `pip install types-colorama types-setuptools`)

> Essentially, I think it's correct to forward the message equally no matter it's initiated in Thread stack or from Host. (@bukepo may have comments on it) So, this PR is a step in that direction.

Even if we argue that it may be correct to do so (and that it is up to next layer to filter messages), I think it will be a change to existing behavior and it may have impact (may have some unintended consequences). I suggest that we try to be careful about this.

I tried digging into older internal bugs/Jiras on what issues we saw related to this (if/when we allowed msgs from host to br passed back to the host):
- One use-case was to use thread netif (wpan0) as a low priority default route.
   - The use-case was, for example if WiFi is connected on device, we use that for all traffic (e.g. traffic to a service/cloud), but if WiFi is down, then we allow Thread netif to be used (in hope that the traffic may be able to routed in Thread mesh to a BR which can then forward it to the intended destination). 
   - That's where the loop issue could happen.
   - We can argue that such a use may not be valid/allowed (with new BR model) and/or we need additional filtering (e.g. ingress filtering) to allow this. But would be good to consider it.
- I recall there were some issues related to ICMP redirect msgs (host/tun netif sending them down to OT and when passed back causes some issue on host side itself) - I think we added filtering in `wpantund` for this.
- I recall we saw similar (looping of msg) issues on RTOS type of device which used lwip for IP stack.

* First: Create a redirector
```python
import sys


class ConsoleOutputRedirect:
    """ Wrapper to redirect stdout or stderr """

    def __init__(self, fp):
        self.fp = fp

    def write(self, s):
        self.fp.write(s)

    def writelines(self, lines):
        self.fp.writelines(lines)

    def flush(self):
        self.fp.flush()


stdout_redirect = ConsoleOutputRedirect(sys.stdout)
```

* Second: Use StringIO to keep the sys.stdout
```python
def test_bar_render_zeppelin():
    CurrentConfig.NOTEBOOK_TYPE = NotebookType.ZEPPELIN
    c = (
        Bar()
        .add_xaxis(["A", "B", "C"])
        .add_yaxis("series0", [1, 2, 4])
        .add_yaxis("series1", [2, 3, 6])
    )
    # Block Console stdout
    output_buffer = StringIO()
    stdout_redirect.fp = output_buffer
    temp_stdout = sys.stdout
    sys.stdout = stdout_redirect

    # render
    c.render_notebook()
    sys.stdout = temp_stdout

    # Block Result
    assert_in("%html", output_buffer.getvalue())
```

Sourcemap seems very complicated and providing very little benefit over doing it the way I suggest. You can't catch every error at parse time and I don't see that as a problem even a little bit. You can do a semantic review later with dramatically less effort than trying to build a sourcemap for Python primitive objects.

"Task foo does not have a class_path" doesn't tie back to a particular source file anyhow.

I did the sourcemap thing in Snowfakery because the benefit there was large but as you probably remember and have noticed, line number handling had to suffuse the whole code base. It was a lot of work and I don't see the cost/benefit the same way in CumulusCI. We'd need to identify WHICH errors can only be found after merge AND validate that they actually even relate to a single file as opposed to the project as a whole.

To fix this I went with early error reporitng approach rather than skipping the `if (params->inject_missing_terminator)` block and letting `if (!params->valid(s, maxlen))` block report the error.

I'm treating it as a terminal state for replica start up for accurate replica state tracking. 

> It seems like all we need to do here is just count the number of replica failures that occur

That's exactly what we want to track, but the implementation matters. My initial attempt was to simply put them at stopped / should stop state, for non-transient failures we will think we never reached desirable # of replicas thus try to spin up replicas forever. Just counting raw # of retries is also not correct since it could vary on each replica.

So essentially what we need is be able to count # of replicas failed to start with no retires left, but also don't confuse them with the rest so new update() cycle won't try to move or loose track on them

> I agree with the drawbacks you've identified. We had a discussion earlier about the question of having OpenThread maintain a pool, and we had decided not to do that. The deciding argument being that maintaining a pool in OpenThread would require OpenThread to be re-compiled if the pool size is changed, but that's undesirable in certain situations.

I know we discussed this and I do see that you already spent the time to find a solution/code to make it work but I'd still think it will be good for us to re-visit it.

I think different solutions may address all situations, and longer term having `TcpEndPoint` be an internal definition (i.e., not having it as `otTcpEndPoint` in public header) will help simplify things.

The key drawback for me (in addition to complexity it adds) is that in this model, we may end up over-estimating the size (use larger than necessary buffers for `otTcpEndPoint`) which is not a good for memory-contained devices.

I don't want us to penalize the memory-constrained case in favor of flexibility for the not-constrained case.

Here is my view on this:

- On a memory-constrained device, one way or another, the app developer needs to determine the the max number of TCP end points that may be needed. I think this would apply independent of whether it is being managed by app layer code or we use a pool and have an OT configuration to specify the pool size.
   - And as I said there are already other items (like max number of unicast/multicast address) that have a hard max limit which is determined by an  `OPENTRHEAD_CONFIG` and if the app need changes, we need to re-build OT stack.

- If we need flexibility and support for dynamic number of end-points (which I guess makes sense on a not-very-memory constrained platform), then
  1) We can still use a pool with large enough estimate for number of entries that cover the application use-cases.
      - Even if the pool size needs a change, requiring a re-build of the OT stack should not add a lot of overhead.
  2) We can have an option (under certain `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG`) where OT stack uses heap to allocate the `TcpEndPoint` instance instead of having a pool.
      - This way it is more dynamic and tied to platform (heap size available)
      - For example, this makes sense for POSIX like platform where we can have way more memory available and can use heap.
   3) We can also consider adding a third option (again under some `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG`) where we still require the user to provide the buffer for the TCP end points but instead of defining the `otTcpEndPoint` struct in public API, we have an API where the user can ask and get the size of the buffer needed for a TCP end-point object. Then we expect the caller to provide a buffer of at least that size to us (which we then use `new` to convert to a `TcpEndPoint` instance).

Thoughts?

Random thought - how to we treat strings like the following?
```
SELECT "%%(name:type)s" as weird_literal;
```

Percent-formatted strings treat double percent signs as a literal percent and would keep the following string intact:
```py
>>> 'SELECT "%%(name)s" as weird_literal;' % {"name": "foo"}
'SELECT "%(name)s" as weird_literal;'
```
Presumably our extension should follow suit for consistency, and would thus benefit from a negative lookbehind assertion?
```py
r"(?<!%)%(\(([^:)]*)(:(\w+))?\))?s"
```

If we strictly stick to the new [BC policy](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/wiki/PyTorch's-Python-Frontend-Backward-and-Forward-Compatibility-Policy), we can only remove if the warning includes the version this is going to be removed. So the kNN query and the interpolation were later moved out of the traced function (see [this commit](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/pull/1062/commits/b9592d44cf638925052adf20a9b79f4e3472f997)).

For your mixture comment, what I expect to happen is that only the "manually" added dataset's sensor would be used. There is no reason that a composite from the manually-added sensor's composite YAML couldn't use a dataset that comes from the readers. However, if the reader's sensor's composite YAML is the one with the composite that depends on both then it wouldn't work.

I'm not sure the best way around this. The Scene either:

1. Never consults the reader
2. Always consults the reader
3. Only consults the reader if no datasets are loaded

I'm not sure if there are any other options that are consistent and not prone to mistakes/bugs. Any other ideas?
